coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots modified Bi2MoO6 microspheres as anode materials for high performance aqueous rechargeable nickel//bismuth batteries

coredata.dc:description: Aqueous rechargeable batteries, as one kind of new energy storage and conversion systems, have gradually drawn researchers’ concern. Herein, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are used as conductive agents to prepare multidimensional Bi2MoO6@N-CDs anode materials. The synergistic effect between three-dimensional (3D) flowering spherical Bi2MoO6 and zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots derived carbon depends on the spatial and compositional diversity. The anode performs excellent electrochemical properties owing to the introduction of N-CDs offering high density of reaction sites and quick charge transfer process. Similar to Bi2MoO6@N-CDs, N‑CDs modified Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 was prepared as cathode materials, manifesting excellent specific capacity. A novel aqueous rechargeable nickel//bismuth battery with above materials delivers the energy density of 50.85 Wh/kg along with power density of 344.27 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. The present research would broaden the path for effectively structuring of electrode active materials for aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots modified Bi2MoO6 microspheres as anode materials for high performance aqueous rechargeable nickel//bismuth batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous rechargeable batteries, as one kind of new energy storage and conversion systems, have gradually drawn researchers’ concern. Herein, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are used as conductive agents to prepare multidimensional Bi2MoO6@N-CDs anode materials. The synergistic effect between three-dimensional (3D) flowering spherical Bi2MoO6 and zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots derived carbon depends on the spatial and compositional diversity. The anode performs excellent electrochemical properties owing to the introduction of N-CDs offering high density of reaction sites and quick charge transfer process. Similar to Bi2MoO6@N-CDs, N‑CDs modified Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 was prepared as cathode materials, manifesting excellent specific capacity. A novel aqueous rechargeable nickel//bismuth battery with above materials delivers the energy density of 50.85 Wh/kg along with power density of 344.27 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. The present research would broaden the path for effectively structuring of electrode active materials for aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the environmental pollution caused by the continuous combustion of fossil energy, how to store and convert green yet intermittent renewable energy (e.g., wind, solar, tidal energy, biomass, geothermy, etc.) is becoming a priority in academic community to realize the strategic goal of “emission peak and carbon neutrality” [1–10]. Among the multitudinous energy storage devices, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are deemed as one successfully commercial energy storage device originated from the superiority such as high energy density and small self-discharge. Nevertheless, the scarcity and uneven distribution of lithium resources hinder the further large-scale application of LIBs. In recent years, battery safety issues based on the strong volatility, toxicity and flammability of organic electrolytes (e.g., Boeing 787 battery fires in 2013, Samsung Note 7 explosions in 2016, and the Tesla Model S combustions in 2019 etc.) have stimulated the pursuit of aqueous rechargeable batteries (ARBs) with the preponderance in terms of long-term safety, excellent ionic conductivity, cost-effectiveness and tolerance against mishandling [11].

Novel aqueous rechargeable nickel//bismuth (Ni//Bi) battery, as a rising star in the battery system, has been attracting much more attentions because of the superiority of excellent energy/power density, good conductivity, highly intrinsic safety along with reduced production cost [10–13]. Bismuth-based materials, as anode materials for aqueous rechargeable batteries, has only been studied successively until 2015 [12–16]. Nowadays, in terms of aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi battery, bismuth based anode active materials are mainly focused on Bi, Bi2O3, Bi2Se3 and their composites with carbon materials [8,17,18], while cathode active materials are also limited to Ni(OH)2, NiO, NiCo2O4, CoNi2S4 etc. [19,20]. It is well known that the electrochemical performance of Ni//Bi batteries is closely correlated with the properties of electrode active materials [6,21]. Designing electrode active materials with porous structure, more active sites and high electronic conductivity is beneficial for achieving good electron transfer, accelerating electrolyte ion diffusion, further improving the electrochemical performances [22,23]. Hence, developing both anode and cathode active materials with those above properties for aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries is of great importance, but still faces great challenges [24–26].

Bi2MoO6 with features such as unique layered crystal structure, good thermal stability and non-toxicity, is one n-type semiconducting materials and broadly utilized as photocatalyst for environmental purification [27–32]. Moreover, the feasibility of Bi2MoO6 as electrode materials for supercapacitors and batteries in different potential ranges and electrolytes has also been evaluated. As reported, Bi2MoO6 can exhibit relatively excellent electrochemical properties in KOH electrolyte within negative potential window yet often used as cathode active materials for supercapacitors [33–35]. To date, the research on Bi2MoO6 as anode materials for Ni//Bi batteries is still scarce. Based on our previous work [36], the prepared highly porous Bi2MoO6 microspheres as anode materials, manifest good specific capacity of 163.2 mAh/g at 1 A/g and excellent rate performance of 79.6 % in 1 ∼ 15 A/g, suggesting the feasibility of Bi2MoO6 as anode materials for aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries. However, the electrochemical performances (such as the specific capacity, rate and cycling performance) of Bi2MoO6 based anode for practical application are still hampered by its relatively poor electronic conductivity, which could bring about constrained charge transfer. What’s more, structure degradation along with the loss of active materials and electronic connection during electrochemical reaction process could also give rise to the electrochemical performance attenuation after cycling.

In order to effectively mitigate those difficulties, hybridizing Bi2MoO6 with well-designed micromorphology and structure with carbon materials (such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers etc.) is considered as one powerful approach to improve the electrochemical performances, because of which could enhance the electronic conductivity, regulate the surface chemistry and maintain the structure integrity of electrode active materials [37–40]. Whereas, there will exist new problems, such as the weak binding interface, cumbersome synthesizing procedure, and reducing capacity of electrode after excessive carbon content introduced. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) with sizes below 10 nm, consisting of quasi-discrete spherical cores and surface functional groups, have been widely used in the field of energy storage because of their unique nanostructure and excellent electrical conductivity [41–43]. Especially, doping heteroatom (such as N atom) into CDs could further significantly improve electronic conductivities, modify the electronic structure and increase the heterostructure interface interaction [44]. N atoms are reported to have a higher electronegativity than C atoms (3.04 vs 2.55) and can destroy the π-electron inertia of C atoms through electron transfer, thereby exciting the activity of the neighboring positively charged Sp2-C and providing more active sites. Compared to above other carbon materials, CDs exhibit superior dispersibility into various solvents and high reactivity related to their rich surface functional groups and smaller size, contributing to function as connectors and substrate to construct multifarious heterostructures [45]. For example, N, S co-doped CQDs‑decorated NiCo2S4 composite as cathode materials delivered excellent electrochemical performances thanks to the synergistic effect between N, S‑codoped CQDs and unique micro‑/nanostructured NiCo2S4 with urchin‑like micromorphology [44]. Therefore, it is sensibly anticipated that integrating N doped CDs into Bi2MoO6 could strengthen their electrochemical properties.

As discussed above, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs was prepared as anode materials for aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi battery. Owing to the synergistic effect between three-dimensional (3D) flowering spherical Bi2MoO6 and zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots depends on the spatial and compositional diversity, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 possesses obviously enhanced electrochemical properties with a high specific capacity of 212.3 mAh/g at 1 A/g, rate performance of 72.7 % in 1 ∼ 20 A/g and capacity retention of 42 % over 1500 cycles at 10 A/g. Meanwhile, N-CDs were also hybridized with Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 as cathode active materials. With the optimal dosage mass of N-CDs, the cathode material (Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8) exhibits a specific capacity of 176.9 mAh/g at 1 A/g. The corresponding aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi battery achieves an energy density of 50.85 Wh/kg along with power density of 344.27 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. This research would broaden the path for the effective structuring of electrode active materials for aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries.

N-CDs was synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Specifically, 0.94 g urea and 1.0 g citric acid were dissolved into 20 mL N, N-dimethylformamide under stirring and followed by ultrasonic processing. And then, the above solution was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 20 min. After cooling down, the as-prepared solution was dialyzed for 48 h with Molecular Weight Cut Off of 3500 Da. Finally, the dialyzed material was freezing-dried and reground for further use.

For the preparation of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs, N-CDs (2, 2.5 and 3 mg) were first added into 60 mL ethanol, respectively. After homogeneous dispersion under intense ultrasound, 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.5 mmol of Na2MoO4·2H2O were dispersed in the above solution and continually sonicated for 20 min. After that, 20 mL ethylene glycol was added and subjected to ultrasonication and stirring. And then, the solution was treated hydrothermally at 170 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the as-collected samples in autoclave were rinsed with ethanol and deionized water thoroughly, and then dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. According to the mass of N-CDs, the corresponding anode active materials were designated as Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X (X = 2, 2.5, and 3). For comparison, pure Bi2MoO6 was also prepared without adding N-CDs. The mean mass loading of anode active material is 3.44 mg/cm2.

For the synthesis of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs, different masses of N-CDs (6, 8 and 10 mg) were dispersed in 70 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min to disperse homogenously. Afterwards, 1 mmol Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O, 1 mmol Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O, 2 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O and 12 mmol hexamethylenetetramine were dissolved into the above mixed solution in turn, and then they were treated hydrothermally at 150 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the as-collected samples were filtered, washed and dried. Finally, the as-prepared samples were further annealed at 400 °C for 1 h with ramp rate of 5 °C/min in N2 atmosphere. The samples prepared with different mass of N-CDs were named as Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X, where “X” stands for the mass of N-CDs. The mean mass loading of cathode active materials is 2.96 mg/cm2.

The structure, surface elemental constituent, micro-morphology and pore structure of active materials were characterized by X-ray diffraction (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher Electron Corporation, USA), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 670, USA), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS, Germany), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X, Czech), Quantachrome Autosorb-iQ-MP analyzer (ASAP 2460 3.01, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements of electrode active materials
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical performances of electrode active materials were evaluated by three-electrode system using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760e) at room temperature, where the electrochemical test means include cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The working electrode were prepared by mixing electrode active materials with acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) with the mass ratio of 8:1:1 coated onto nickel foam followed by vacuum drying under 100 ℃ for 10 h and pressed under the pressure of 10 MPa. What’s more, Hg/HgO double salt bridge, platinum sheet and 6 M KOH aqueous solution were used as the reference electrode, counter electrode and electrolyte, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly and measurement of the Ni//Bi battery
ce:section.ce:para: The Ni//Bi battery model was assembled by using Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 as anode active materials (1 cm × 1 cm), Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 as cathode active materials (1 cm × 1 cm), separator and 6 M KOH solution as electrolyte within the PTFE model. Those electrodes were pre-activated for 100 cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The total mass of electrode active materials for this Ni//Bi battery model is approximately 9.84 mg.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were operated in CP2K/Quickstep package. A hybrid Gaussian and plane-wave formalism with the pseudopotential approximation (double-ζ MOLOPT basis sets (DZVP-MOLOPT-SR-GTH) and Goedecker-Teter-Hutter (GTH) pseudopotentials) were employed. And the cutoff energy of 450 Ry was set for the plane-wave basis set. All the structures were relaxed until the forces became less than 0.05 eV/Å with the convergence condition of 1.0 × 10−5.

XRD was carried out first to identify the phase evolution of anode active materials after modified with N-CDs. As exhibited in Fig. 1
a, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X possess the similar diffraction pattern to Bi2MoO6, and the distinguishable diffraction peaks at 28.2°, 32.5°, 47.1° and 55.5° can be assigned to the (131), (200), (260) and (331) crystal planes of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 84–0787), respectively [36]. No obvious diffraction peaks of N-CDs can be observed, owing to the low content of N-CDs with poor diffraction peak intensity. FT-IR patterns of N-CDs, Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 are shown in Fig. 1 b. For N-CDs, the intense peak located at 3404 cm−1 can be attributed to C-OH and N-H bond, the band at 1655 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending vibration of C=O, and the peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1132 cm−1 belong to N-H and C-NH-C band [46]. For Bi2MoO6, the observed peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 726 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of Mo-O and Mo-O-Mo bond of Bi2MoO6, respectively. And the spectral band at about 614 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching and deformation mode of the Bi-O bond. Moreover, the peak at 3600 ∼ 3300 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of -OH, and the peak located at 1630 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of water molecules [36,47]. After introducing N-CDs, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs exhibits similar peaks, but the peaks shift toward higher wavenumber, and the absorption intensity of the peaks is higher than Bi2MoO6, suggesting N-CDs and Bi2MoO6 have been successfully composited [46,48].

The XPS full spectrums of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 in Fig. 2
a illustrate the presence of Bi, Mo, C, O and N elements. The high-resolution spectrum of Bi 4f consists of two peaks with binding energy of 159.2, 164.5 eV, corresponding to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which suggests the element Bi exists in the form of Bi3+ (Fig. 2 b) [14]. Two characteristic peaks at 232.3 and 235.5 eV can be attributed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively, indicating Mo exists in the + 6 oxidation. (Fig. 2 c) [26]. As for O 1 s spectrum, two peaks at 529.84 (O1) and 530.37 eV (O2) can be associated with Bi-O bond and Mo-O, respectively. Compared to pure Bi2MoO6, one peak at 532.1 eV (O3) suggests the successful formation of Bi-O-C (Fig. 2 d) [49]. Meanwhile, the presence of Bi-O-C bond demonstrates the strong electron interaction in Bi2MoO6@N-CDs heterointerface, which may effectively accelerate electronic transfer and enhance structural stability. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 e) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.09, and 288.33 eV, which can be assigned to sp2 hybridized carbon (C=C), C-O and carbonyl carbon (C=O), respectively [50,51]. Notably, the oxygen-containing functional groups inherited from N-CDs may be beneficial in the formation of reliable heterogeneous interfaces. From N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 f), three species of nitrogen-containing functional groups at 396.29, 398.08, and 399.83 eV are detected, associated with pyridyl nitrogen (396.29, 398.08 eV) and pyrrole nitrogen (399.83 eV), which exhibits strong affinity and contributes to the formation of heterogeneous interfaces [18]. Additionally, the peak positions of Bi, Mo and O in Bi2MoO6@N-CDs composites shifted toward higher binding energies compared with pure Bi2MoO6, indicating the change of electronic structure after Bi2MoO6 was modified with N-CDs. The above results show that Bi2MoO6 was successfully modified with N-CDs.

The pristine Bi2MoO6 exhibits flower-like microsphere morphology with irregular particles, and the microsphere is composed of many nanosheets. The lattice spacing with 0.31 nm and 0.16 nm, corresponds to (131) and (331) planes of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 84–0787), respectively. Moreover, the gaps between adjacent nanosheets and open spaces from the stacking of Bi2MoO6 microspheres may account for the developed pore structure (Fig. S1), facilitating to migration of electrolyte ions and accessing of active sites to participate in the electrochemical reaction. After modified with N-CDs, the microsphere morphology can be well retained, while the nanosheets assembled for microspheres become thinner, which may be related to the inductive effect of N-CDs as nucleation substrate. Meanwhile, the TEM images further confirm the microsphere structures of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 assembled from ultra-thin nanosheets, matching well with the SEM images (Fig. 3
b ∼ c). As displayed in Fig. 3 d, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 possesses significant lattice spacing of 0.30 nm in region 2, which can be assigned to (131) plane of Bi2MoO6, and 0.26 nm in region 1 can be indexed to the (020) plane of graphite crystallite, reflecting N-CDs nanoparticles are successfully introduced into the Bi2MoO6 as heterogeneous structure. Moreover, the disordered lattice fringes can be attributed to the doping of N-CDs (the red arrow in region 2), suggesting N-CDs are effectively anchored in the surface of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets [45]. In addition, a series of diffraction rings are shown in SAED pattern, corresponding to the (131), (062) and (331) planes of Bi2MoO6, which agree well with the results of XRD (Fig. S2). And corresponding elemental mappings (Fig. 3 e) confirm the presence of Bi, Mo, O, C and N, further proving the successful complexation of Bi2MoO6 and N-CDs, which is also consistent with XPS results. And the uniform distribution of C also proves the successful introduction of N-CDs. Additionally, the SEM images of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-3 can be observed in Fig. S3. Notably, Bi2MoO6 possesses three-dimensional (3D) flower-like microsphere morphology formed by interlocking two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets. So, the synergistic effect between three-dimensional (3D) flowering spherical Bi2MoO6 and zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots depends on the spatial and compositional diversity, providing stable backbone with high number of active sites and multi-channel electron transport paths, which can facilitate the unobstructed diffusion of electrolyte ions and increase the utilization of active materials.

As depicted in Fig. 4 , the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms exhibit type-IV curves combined with an H3-type hysteresis loop, and the hysteresis loop become more evident with increasing of N-CDs content. The specific surface area and total pore volume of pristine Bi2MoO6 are 30.2 m2/g and 0.168 cm3/g, respectively. The introduction of N-CDs impacts the specific surface area and total pore volume of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X, which are 52.9 m2/g and 0.175 cm3/g for Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2, 56.9 m2/g and 0.202 cm3/g for Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5, 51.9 m2/g and 0.214 cm3/g for Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-3. The specific surface areas of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X are also higher than other Bi2MoO6-based active materials, such as flower-like Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres (44.6 m2/g) [33], nanosheet-like Bi2MoO6 (8.8 m2/g) [52], two-dimensional Bi2MoO6 nanosheet@rGO composites (15.54 m2/g) [53], carbon sphere@nano petal-like Bi2MoO6 hybrid (37.1 m2/g) [54], which is helpful to provide the increased active sites to store energy.

Fig. 5 shows the electrochemical performances of as-prepared anode active materials Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X. Significant redox peaks can be observed in the CV curves at the scan rate of 5 mV/s (Fig. 5 a), demonstrating the energy storage mainly relies on Faradic redox reaction [55]. Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X possess higher enclosed area of CV curves than pristine Bi2MoO6, suggesting the electrochemical performance can be effectively improved by introducing N-CDs. Especially, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 presents the highest current density response. As shown in Fig. 5 c, two oxidation peaks (Peak1 and Peak3) and one reduction peak (Peak2) can be observed in CV curves of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 at various scan rates. The redox peaks move towards two ends of potential window, respectively, and do not appear visible shape distortion, indicating highly reproducible redox process and rate performance. The semi-quantitative analysis was carried out on the basis of the formula i(v) = k1v + k2v1/2 (k1v and k2v1/2 stand for the surface capacitive charge and the diffusion-controlled charge, respectively) to further clarify the electrochemical kinetic behavior of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 [12,56]. The fitted b-values for Peak1, Peak2 and Peak3 were obtained as b1 = 0.52162, b2 = 0.5442 and b3 = 0.90643, respectively (Fig. 5 d), suggesting the electrochemical behavior of Bi2MoO6 based anode active materials in the 6 M KOH electrolyte is mainly controlled by the diffusion-controlled process, which is similar to bismuth-based anode active materials (such as Bi, Bi2O2CO3, Bi2O3, Bi2WO6 etc.) worked in KOH electrolyte. The GCD curves of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X at 1A/g are compared in Fig. 5 b. Conspicuous charge and discharge platforms correspond to the redox peaks on the CV curves, further suggesting that Bi2MoO6 based anode active materials belongs to typical battery-type material. Fig. 5 f displays GCD curves of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 at different current densities, apparent charge–discharge voltage platform can be observed. Moreover, the average specific capacity of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X gradually decreases with increasing of current density, and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 possesses the largest average specific capacity at every single current density. The specific discharge capacity of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 reaches up to 212.3, 196.5, 187.3, 181.1, 175.5, 172.5, 167.1, 164.4, 160.6 and 154.4 mAh/g at the current density of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 15 and 20 A/g, respectively (Fig. 5 g). Additionally, the CV and GCD curves of Bi2MoO6, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2 together with Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-3 can be seen in Fig. S4. And the specific capacity of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X as electrodes at different current densities are shown in Table 1 . As indicated in Fig. 5 h, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 possesses a cycling property with 42 % capacity retention at 10A/g after 1500 cycles, which is comparable to Bi2O3/activated carbon (59 % after 1000 cycles) [57] and sponge-like Bi2O3 (50 % after 1500 cycles) [58]. EIS tests are employed and results are exhibited in Fig. 5 i. The Nyquist diagram of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X consists of a semicircle and a solid axis intercept, representing the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the internal resistance (Rs) respectively. The Rs value of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 (Rs≈0.76 Ω) suggests that Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-X, as anode active materials, possess good conductivity and fast charge transfer ability. Consequently, the superior electrochemical performance may be attributed to the unique 3D porous flower-microsphere structure, which can promote the penetration of electrolyte ions and effectively enhance the electrochemical utilization of active materials.

Ex-situ XRD and XPS was used to investigate the electrochemical reaction mechanism of Bi2MoO6. The XRD characterized the phase evolution of Bi2MoO6 at different charging/discharging states, as exhibit in Fig. S5. The alteration of diffraction peaks suggests the phase change in the process of charging and discharging, further confirming the Faraday redox reactions take place. The diffraction pattern of anode accords well with Bi2MO6 before electrochemical test (state (0)). From the results of XRD, we can know that Bi2MO6 is first converted to bismuth metal, then bismuth metal gradually converts to Bi2O3 during the charging process (state (1 ∼ 3)), and as the discharge proceeding Bi2O3 is gradually turned into bismuth metal (state (4 ∼ 6)). Additionally, the binding energies of Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 gradually shift towards lower binding energies in the process of charging, followed by a gradual move towards higher binding energies during discharging (Fig. S5 c). As exhibited in Fig. S5 d, the atomic percentage of Mo gradually decreases from 3.1 at. % (state (0)) to 0.11 at. % (state (6)), indicating that Mo is not involved in the electrochemical reaction. The O 1 s spectrum can be fitted to two peaks O1 and O2, corresponding to the bismuth-oxygen bond and oxygen in the water molecule, respectively. (Fig. S5 e). And the area of bismuth-oxygen bond first increases and then decreases during the electrochemical reaction. The XPS results agree well with ex-XRD.

A possible electrochemical reaction occurring on the reduction peak (peak 2) can be expressed as follows [33,35,36,59]:

From the point view of atomic, to further understand how the Bi2MoO6@N-CDs heterogeneous interface improves its electrical conductivity, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used. Firstly, the crystal structure diagrams of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs are shown in Fig. 6
a. Work function was conducted to further explore the electrical conductivity of Bi2MoO6@N-CDs. Compared to Bi2MoO6, the value of work function decreases from approximately 4.91 eV to 4.69 V due to the introduction of N-CDs (Fig. 6 b), suggesting the formation of heterogeneous interface between Bi2MoO6 and N-CDs can efficiently enhance the electron transport and improve its electrochemical properties[48]. The density of states (DOS) of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs are employed in Fig. 6 c ∼ e. A significant band gap (≈1.27 eV) exists between the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) in Bi2MoO6, however, Bi2MoO6@N-CDs possesses 0.8 eV band gap near the Fermi level, indicating the heterogeneous interface between Bi2MoO6 and N-CDs can effectively improve the electron transport (Fig. 6 c) [2,60]. Moreover, the orbitals of O split at around 0.5 eV during the formation of the interface, and interact with C and Bi (Fig. 6 d ∼ e), resulting in an extension of the energy band structure and a significant increase in the charge response. The charge density plots of Bi2MoO6 and Bi2MoO6@N-CDs are shown in Fig. S6. From the 2D slice of charge density (Fig. S7), it can be seen that the charge distribution also becomes more homogeneous during the formation of the heterogeneous interface, mainly through covalent bonding interactions. Furthermore, the charge transfer behavior between the heterogeneous interfaces of Bi2MoO6 and N-CDs was further analyzed by charge density difference. As displayed in Fig. 6 f, charge accumulation (yellow) and charge depletion (green) with a large overlap can be observed between Bi, O and C, demonstrating the presence of Bi-O-C bonds effectively enhance the charge transfer between the Bi2MoO6@N-CDs heterogeneous interface. Additionally, the degree of charge response significantly increases between the Bi2MoO6 and N-CDs heterogeneous interfaces along the Z-axis [12,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and electrochemical properties of the cathode materials
ce:section.ce:para:
Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X were designed as cathode active materials for the Ni//Bi battery. As seen from Fig. 7
a, the diffraction patterns of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs are similar to that of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 in our previous work. And the characteristic peaks of N-CDs can also not be observed, reflecting the low content of N-CDs with poor diffraction peak intensity [36]. As exhibited in Fig. 7 b, the peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of −OH and water molecules, respectively. And the peaks located at 400–1000 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the M−O bond, which M stands for Co, Ni and Mo. The XPS survey spectrum of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 in Fig. 7 c proves the presence of Co, Ni, Mo, O, C and N elements. The Co 2p spectrum shown in Fig. 7 d display four peaks, which can be assigned to Co 2p3/2 (the main peak at 780.66 eV together with the satellite peak at 785.78 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (the main peak located at 796.53 eV and the satellite peak located at 802.46 eV), indicating the element Co is present in the divalent oxidation state [61]. Similar to Co 2p, two peaks centered at 855.24 and 872.93 eV can be identified as the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p 1/2, together with the accompanying satellite peaks located at 861.27 and 879.42 eV, respectively, demonstrating Ni is present in the Ni2+ (Fig. 7 e) [55]. For the Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. 7 f), the main peak at 232.16 eV and the satellite peak at 230.39 eV are related to Mo 3d5/2, and the main peak at 235.31 eV and the satellite peak at 233.48 eV can be designated to Mo 3d3/2, indicating the element Mo exists in the Mo6+ state [36]. The O 1 s spectrum possesses four peaks at 529.82, 530.69, 531.56 and 532.79 eV, matching perfectly with metal-O, O-H, O-C=O bond and H2O, respectively (Fig. 7 g) [62]. From the spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 7 h), the nitrogen can be fitted into three species, including pyridyl nitrogen (395.93 and 397.89 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (399.6 eV) [38]. From C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7 i), three types of carbon-containing bond at 284.78, 286.16 and 288.28 eV are detected, which can be assigned to sp2 hybridized carbon (C=C), C-O and C=O bond [36,63]. The above results further prove the formation of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 heterostructure.

As displayed in Fig. 8 , the introduction of N-CDs obviously changes the micro-morphology of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4. Original porous microsphere structure disappeared, the irregular and fluffy granular morphology is presented. Additionally, the “ribbon-like” lamellae intertwine to form a porous structure. TEM images also confirm Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 possesses irregular morphology, and the heterogeneous structure is thicker in the middle parts and more transparent at the edge portions, agreeing well with SEM images. (Fig. 8 b and c). HRTEM image (Fig. 8 d) presents high crystallinity with clear lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, associated with the (132) plane of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4. Fig. 8 e displays the SAED of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8, and the different diffraction rings prove its polycrystalline characteristic [64]. The corresponding EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 8 f) reveals that the element of Co, Ni, Mo O, N and C are distributed relatively homogenously. The SEM images of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-6 together with Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-10 can be observed in Fig. S8, both of them exhibit irregular and granular morphology, combined with porous structure, further suggesting the micro-morphology of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 change owing to the introduction of N-CDs.

Fig. 9 illustrates the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution profiles of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X. The specific surface area and total pore volume are 141.8 m2/g and 0.486 cm3/g for Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-6, 159.4 m2/g and 0.492 cm3/g for Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 and 157.5 m2/g and 0.548 cm3/g for Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-10, respectively. Additionally, the specific surface area of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X are smaller than the pristine Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 (293 m2/g, 0.8997 cm3/g), but still larger than hollow NiMoO4 nanorods (37.79 m2/g) [65], NiMoO4 (39.8 m2/g) [66], PCNS@NiMoO4 (99.0 m2/g) [66] and NiMoO4-rGO (50.8 m2/g) [67]. The results above demonstrate introducing N-CDs can impact the micro-morphology and the specific surface area and pore size distribution.

The CV curves of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X at the scan rate of 5 mV/s are shown in Fig. 10
a. The significant redox peaks indicate that energy storage of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X primarily dependent on the Faradic redox reaction. As depicted in Fig. 10 b, the GCD curves of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X at 1 A/g possess obvious charge/discharge voltage platform, corresponding to the redox peak on the CV curves. Moreover, Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 exhibits the longest voltage platform, suggesting the introduction of N-CDs efficiently enhances the utilization of active materials together with improves the specific capacity. As displayed in Fig. 10 c, the CV curves exhibit similar shapes, suggesting good Faradic response and rate performance. The Faradaic battery behavior is derived from reversible redox reactions according to following equation [68]:
(9)
C

0.5 O H

Additionally, the redox peaks shift towards the ends of the voltage window and the current peak increases as increasing the scan rates, indicating Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-X display good electrochemical reaction kinetics. The fitting lines of i/v1/2 to v1/2 of the Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 is illustrated in Fig. 10 d, and the b values are calculated to be b1 = 0.91198, b2 = 0.72095 respectively, indicating electrochemical behavior of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 as cathode active material in 6 M electrolyte is controlled by the combination of diffusion-controlled processes and surface capacitance effects [12]. The contribution ratio of capacitive and diffusion-controlled charge versus scan rate for Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 are depicted in Fig. 10 e. The CV curves displays that the capacity contributions of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 are 71.31 %, 71.54 %, 72.54 %, 73.62 %, 74.51 %, 76.85 %, 83.19 % and 91.12 % at 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8 and 10 mV/s, respectively, which is larger than that of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 (eg: 66.7 % at 5 mV/s, 76.3 % at 10 mV/s). The above results further suggest the introduction of N-CDs can improve the electrochemical kinetic [44]. Moreover, the CV and GCD curves of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-6 together with Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-10 can be seen in Fig. S9. As exhibited in Fig. 10 f, the specific capacity of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@rGO-X exhibits a gradual decay trend with the current density increasing. And Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 displays the highest average specific capacity at every single current density, which are 178.8, 172.2, 166.1, 160.1, 154.2, 148.6, 137.8, 127.5 and 105.4 mAh/g at different current densities of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 15 A/g, respectively. And the corresponding rate performance is up to 58.72 %, which may be attributed to the fact that the introduction of N-CDs can enhance the electrical conductivity of electrode materials (Fig. 10 g). Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 as cathode active materials manifest capacity retention of 50 % over 2500 repeated charging/discharging at 10 A/g (Fig. 10 h), less than that of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 but higher than NiMoO4/MWCNTS (66.7 % at 1A/g after 1000 cycles) [69], which may be attributed to the morphology change after introduction of N-CDs. The smallest semicircular diameter and higher angular straight lines of Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 demonstrate that the small internal resistance and fast electrolyte ion diffusion rate of this materials (Fig. 10 i). Based on the above results, hybridizing Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4 with N-CDs is conductive to improve electrochemical performance.

Aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries were assembled with Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 as anode materials and Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs-8 as cathode materials in 6 M KOH to further investigate their practical applications. The stable operating voltage of this Ni//Bi battery could be extended to 1.6 V (Fig. 11
a). The CV curves of the Ni//Bi battery at various scan rates are exhibited in Fig. 11 b, where the well-defined redox peaks even at 50 mV/s can be observed, indicating good rate performance. Fig. 11 c and d display typical GCD curves at different current densities from 0.5 to 10 A/g. Additionally, the corresponding charge/discharge platform moves towards the lower potential with increasing the current density, further confirming its fast-acting characteristics [18]. As exhibited in Fig. 11 e, the Ni//Bi battery provides a high specific capacity of 73.8 mAh/g at 0.5 A/g and 54.8 % of this value is retained at the high current density of 10 A/g (40.4 mAh/g), which may be attributed to the enhanced ion diffusion and charge transfer. Surprisingly, the Ni//Bi battery affords a capacity retention of 43.4 % after 1500 cycles at a current density of 1 A/g, with a corresponding coulomb efficiency of approximately 95.47 % (Fig. 11 f). Based on the Nyquist plots in Fig. 11 g, the quasi-circular diameter and high angle straight lines suggests the battery model delivers a low internal resistance and a fast electrolyte ion diffusion rate. Ragone plots are used to compare the performance of different types of energy storage devices. Our Ni//Bi cell delivers an energy density of 50.85 Wh/kg along with power density of 344.27 W/kg at 0.5 A/g (Fig. 11 h), which is comparable to that of the commercial battery, such as: 34.29 Wh/kg and 329 W/kg of NiO//Bi2O3@C [12] and 21.8 Wh/kg and 660 W/kg of Ni(OH)2@3D Ni//AC SCs [56] etc. [70–77]. The above electrochemical results further confirm the feasibility of this aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi battery as an energy storage device.

In conclusion, N-CDs-decorated flower-like Bi2MoO6 microspheres have been designed using N-CDs as conductive agents, performing superior electrochemical properties with a high specific capacity (212.3 mAh/g at 1 A/g), excellent rate performance (72.7 % at 20 A/g) and good cycle life (42 % over 1500 cycles at 10 A/g). Moreover, the introduction of N-CDs not only increases the surface area but also enriches the active sites for electrochemical reactions. Meanwhile, a novel Ni//Bi battery configuration with superior electrochemical performance has been developed using Bi2MoO6@N-CDs-2.5 as anode materials and Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@N-CDs as cathode materials. Desirably, the Ni//Bi battery can perform excellent energy density of 50.85 Wh/kg along with power density of 344.27 W/kg at 0.5 A/g. Current work offers extremely promising electrode active materials with superior electrochemical properties and broaden the path for the effective structuring of electrode active materials toward new aqueous rechargeable Ni//Bi batteries.
